reasoning:
The question asks whether 'Explosions/Remote violence' in Malaysia for a 30-day period will be more than 10 times the baseline (which is currently 1.000, meaning 0 events occurred in the past 360 days). Thus, for the question to resolve positively, Malaysia would need to experience at least 11 events of 'Explosions/Remote violence' (according to ACLED definitions) strictly within a single 30-day window leading up to the respective resolution dates. 

Malaysia is currently completely at peace internally and externally, experiencing zero instances of bombings or remote violence. We found no indications of organized military insurgency, terrorism campaigns, or conflict spillover that would lead to >10 remote violence incidents in one month. Even adjacent conflicts like the Southern Thailand insurgency consistently reflect bombings in Thai territory, not Malaysia. Any single terrorist attack is unlikely to hit 11+ remote violence/explosion events over a 30-day period unless an unprecedented, sweeping armed conflict spontaneously breaks out. Given how extreme the requirement is, the probability is assigned cleanly at the minimum bound (5%). Even looking 1 year ahead to March 2026, there is no credible pathway modeled for 11+ bombing events per month in Malaysia.

I've set the probabilities for all windows quite low given the sheer threshold needed compared to a solid baseline of 0.
